children.name: Accipiter
children.description: Goshawks and sparrowhawks — the archetypal bird-hunting hawks, with short rounded wings and long tails adapted for rapid manoeuvre through woodland. The Northern Goshawk is the apex forest raptor of the temperate Northern Hemisphere, capable of overtaking prey at speed through dense trees. The Eurasian Sparrowhawk is the most numerous hawk in Britain and Europe, ambushing small birds at garden feeders and woodland edges in an explosive burst of acceleration. Both species show strong reverse sexual dimorphism — females are dramatically larger than males.

children.name: Accipiter gentilis
children.description: The Eurasian goshawk is a species of medium-large bird of prey in the family Accipitridae, a family which also includes other extant diurnal raptors, such as eagles, buzzards and harriers. It was formerly placed in the genus Accipiter. It is a widespread species that inhabits many of the temperate parts of Eurasia.

children.name: Accipiter nisus
children.description: The Eurasian sparrowhawk, also known as the northern sparrowhawk or simply the sparrowhawk, is a small bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. Adult male Eurasian sparrowhawks have bluish grey upperparts and orange-barred underparts; females and juveniles are brown above with brown barring below. The female is up to 25% larger than the male – one of the greatest size differences between the sexes in any bird species.

children.name: Accipiter cooperii
children.description: Cooper's hawk is a medium-sized hawk native to the North American continent and found from southern Canada to Mexico. This species was formerly placed in the genus Accipiter.

children.name: Accipiter striatus
children.description: The sharp-shinned hawk or northern sharp-shinned hawk, colloquially often nicknamed sharpie, is a small hawk, with males being the smallest hawks in the United States and Canada, but with the species averaging larger than some Neotropical species, such as the tiny hawk. The taxonomy is far from resolved, with some authorities considering the southern taxa to represent three separate species, white-breasted hawk, plain-breasted hawk, and rufous-thighed hawk. The American Ornithological Society and some other checklists keep all four species conspecific.

children.name: Accipiter badius
children.description: The shikra is a small bird of prey in the family Accipitridae found widely distributed in Asia and Africa where it is also called the little banded goshawk. The African forms may represent a separate species but have usually been considered as subspecies of the shikra. The shikra is very similar in appearance, as well as behavior, at least to some degree, to other species including the Chinese sparrowhawk, Eurasian goshawk and Eurasian sparrowhawk.

children.name: Accipiter melanoleucus
children.description: The black sparrowhawk, sometimes known as the black goshawk or great sparrowhawk, is a bird of prey belonging to the family Accipitridae. It was formerly placed in the genus Accipiter. It occurs mainly in forest and non-desert areas south of the Sahara, particularly where there are large trees suitable for nesting; favored habitat includes suburban and human-altered landscapes.

children.name: Accipiter superciliosus
children.description: The tiny hawk is a small diurnal bird of prey found primarily around humid forests of the Neotropics. It is primarily a bird-eater, and is known to prey on hummingbirds.

children.name: Accipiter tachiro
children.description: The African goshawk is an African species of bird of prey in the genus Aerospiza. It was formerly placed in the genus Accipiter. The African goshawk was previously sometimes split with the red-chested goshawk of west Africa treated as a separate species.

children.name: Accipiter rufiventris
children.description: The rufous-breasted sparrowhawk, also known as the rufous-chested sparrowhawk and as the red-breasted sparrowhawk, is a species of bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. It is found in Angola, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Eritrea, Eswatini, Ethiopia, Kenya, Lesotho, Malawi, Mozambique, Rwanda, South Africa, South Sudan, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.

children.name: Accipiter virgatus
children.description: The besra, also called the besra sparrowhawk, is a bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. It was formerly placed in the genus Accipiter. The name "besra" is from the Hindi word for the species.

children.name: Accipiter albogularis
children.description: A distinctive pied hawk of Pacific island forests from the Solomons to Fiji.

children.name: Accipiter fasciatus
children.description: The most widespread diurnal raptor in Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Accipiter novaehollandiae
children.description: An Australian and New Guinean forest hawk that uniquely includes a fully white colour morph.

children.name: Accipiter castanilius
children.description: A small Central African rainforest sparrowhawk; chestnut flanks and barred underparts.

children.name: Accipiter nanus
children.description: One of the smallest Accipiter; found only on Sulawesi.

children.name: Accipiter francesiae
children.description: A small sparrowhawk of Madagascar and the Comoros.

children.name: Accipiter melanochlamys
children.description: A highland forest hawk of New Guinea; black mantle with rufous underparts.

children.name: Accipiter poliogaster
children.description: A rare Amazonian hawk; juvenile plumage mimics the Ornate Hawk-Eagle.

children.name: Accipiter collaris
children.description: A small Andean hawk; distinctive white half-collar and barred underparts.

children.name: Accipiter princeps
children.description: A large dark goshawk restricted to the island of New Britain.

children.name: Accipiter trivirgatus
children.description: A medium-sized Asian goshawk with a prominent crest; hunts birds and small mammals in forested foothills.

children.name: Accipiter soloensis
children.description: A migratory sparrowhawk breeding in China and Korea; winters in Southeast Asia; feeds mainly on frogs.

children.name: Accipiter gularis
children.description: A small sparrowhawk of East Asia; migrates to Southeast Asia for winter.

children.name: Accipiter meyerianus
children.description: A large forest goshawk of the Moluccas, New Guinea and the Solomons.

children.name: Accipiter ovampensis
children.description: A small sparrowhawk of southern and East African woodlands; named after the Ovambo region.

children.name: Accipiter butleri
children.description: A small sparrowhawk endemic to the Nicobar Islands; poorly known and Near Threatened.

children.name: Accipiter poliocephalus
children.description: A grey-headed goshawk of New Guinea lowland forests.

children.name: Accipiter cirrocephalus
children.description: A small Australian and New Guinean sparrowhawk; fine rufous barring on underparts.

children.name: Accipiter hiogaster
children.description: A highly variable goshawk of the Moluccas, New Guinea and Solomon Islands; formerly considered conspecific with A. fasciatus.

children.name: Accipiter griseiceps
children.description: A medium-sized goshawk endemic to Sulawesi; grey head and finely barred underparts.

children.name: Accipiter imitator
children.description: A rare hawk of the Solomon Islands; mimics the sympatric Pied Goshawk.

children.name: Accipiter henicogrammus
children.description: A medium-sized goshawk restricted to the northern Moluccas; brown above, barred white below.

children.name: Buteo
children.description: Buzzards — broad-winged, soaring hawks of open and woodland landscapes. The Common Buzzard is the most abundant and widespread raptor in Europe, its mewing, cat-like call a characteristic sound of the upland and woodland edge. Buzzards are versatile and opportunistic hunters, taking rabbits, voles, earthworms, and carrion depending on season and availability.

children.name: Buteo buteo
children.description: The common buzzard is a medium-sized bird of prey with a large range. It is a member of the genus Buteo in the family Accipitridae. The species lives in most of Europe and extends its breeding range across much of the Palearctic as far as northwestern China, far western Siberia and northwestern Mongolia.

children.name: Buteo lagopus
children.description: An Arctic-breeding buzzard that winters in open farmland. Distinguished by feathered legs.

children.name: Buteo jamaicensis
children.description: The most familiar raptor in North America. Its scream is the default 'raptor sound' in film.

children.name: Buteo rufinus
children.description: A large pale buzzard of steppes and semi-deserts from southeastern Europe to Central Asia.

children.name: Buteo swainsoni
children.description: A slender prairie hawk that makes one of the longest migrations of any North American raptor.

children.name: Buteo lineatus
children.description: A boldly patterned hawk of moist lowland forest and riparian woodland in eastern North America.

children.name: Buteo platypterus
children.description: A small, compact hawk that generates some of the largest raptor migration spectacles.

children.name: Buteo albonotatus
children.description: A slender, dark hawk that closely resembles the Turkey Vulture in flight.

children.name: Buteo regalis
children.description: The largest North American hawk, a specialist predator of prairie dogs and ground squirrels.

children.name: Buteo auguralis
children.description: A small, distinctive buzzard of West and Central African forest edges.

children.name: Buteo trizonatus
children.description: A small buzzard endemic to the montane forests of southern Africa.

children.name: Buteo hemilasius
children.description: The largest Asian Buteo, a powerful pale buzzard of the Tibetan Plateau.

children.name: Buteo leucorrhous
children.description: A small, dark hawk of montane forest in the Andes.

children.name: Buteo galapagoensis
children.description: Endemic to the Galapagos Islands; apex predator with no natural enemies.

children.name: Buteo brachyurus
children.description: A compact buteo with a short tail; occurs in dark and light morphs.

children.name: Buteo solitarius
children.description: The only hawk endemic to Hawaii; known as 'Io in Hawaiian.

children.name: Buteo arruens
children.description: A recently split species from the Red-tailed Hawk complex; endemic to Aruba.

children.name: Buteo oreophilus
children.description: A secretive buzzard of East African montane forests.

children.name: Buteo albigula
children.description: A small Andean buzzard; white throat contrasts with grey-brown body.

children.name: Buteo ridgwayi
children.description: A critically endangered hawk endemic to the Dominican Republic; named after Robert Ridgway.

children.name: Buteo nitidus
children.description: A small Neotropical buteo; finely barred grey underparts.

children.name: Buteo ventralis
children.description: A rare buteo of southern Chilean and Argentine forests; rufous tail and dark brown body.

children.name: Aquila
children.description: True eagles — large, powerful raptors of mountains, forests, and open plains. The Golden Eagle is the most widespread eagle in the Northern Hemisphere.

children.name: Aquila chrysaetos
children.description: Scotland holds the majority of the UK breeding population. The golden nape feathers give the bird its name.

children.name: Aquila clanga
children.description: One of Europe's most endangered raptors; fewer than 1,000 pairs remain globally.

children.name: Aquila heliaca
children.description: Heraldic bird of the Habsburg Empire. A conservation success story in central Europe.

children.name: Aquila rapax
children.description: A widespread but declining eagle of African and South Asian savannas.

children.name: Aquila nipalensis
children.description: A large migratory eagle of Central Asian steppes. Endangered due to steep population declines.

children.name: Aquila verreauxii
children.description: A spectacular, jet-black eagle of rocky mountains; specialised almost exclusively on rock hyraxes.

children.name: Aquila gurneyi
children.description: A large, powerful forest eagle of New Guinea and the Maluku Islands.

children.name: Aquila audax
children.description: Australia's largest bird of prey, with a distinctive long, wedge-shaped tail.

children.name: Aquila fasciata
children.description: A powerful medium-large eagle of rocky hillsides and cliffs from the Mediterranean to Southeast Asia.

children.name: Aquila adalberti
children.description: A critically endangered eagle endemic to Spain and Portugal.

children.name: Aquila pomarina
children.description: A medium-sized eagle of European and Asian forests.

children.name: Aquila africana
children.description: A medium-sized forest eagle of West and Central African lowland rainforest.

children.name: Haliaeetus
children.description: Sea eagles — the largest eagles, specialising in fish, waterbirds, and carrion along coastlines, rivers, and lakes.

children.name: Haliaeetus albicilla
children.description: Europe's largest eagle, with a wingspan up to 2.4 m. Extinct in Britain by 1918; reintroduced from Norway.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucocephalus
children.description: The national bird of the United States. DDT caused catastrophic eggshell thinning; recovered after the 1972 ban.

children.name: Haliaeetus pelagicus
children.description: One of the heaviest eagles in the world, with a massive orange-yellow bill. Vulnerable.

children.name: Haliaeetus vocifer
children.description: One of the most iconic birds of Africa, with a far-carrying, yodelling call.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucogaster
children.description: A large, pale-headed sea eagle of coasts from India to Australia.

children.name: Haliaeetus humilis
children.description: A small fish-eating eagle of fast-flowing mountain rivers in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Haliaeetus leucoryphus
children.description: A large, pale-headed sea eagle of Central Asian rivers and lakes. Vulnerable.

children.name: Haliaeetus vociferoides
children.description: Critically Endangered; fewer than 240 individuals remain in coastal western Madagascar.

children.name: Haliaeetus sanfordi
children.description: A large brown-and-white eagle of the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Circus
children.description: Harriers — slender, long-winged, long-tailed hawks that hunt by quartering low over marshland, moorland, and grassland on upswept wings.

children.name: Circus cyaneus
children.description: A Red List species in Britain primarily due to illegal killing on driven grouse moors.

children.name: Circus aeruginosus
children.description: The largest European harrier, breeding in reedbeds and marshes.

children.name: Circus pygargus
children.description: Circus pygargus (Montagu's Harrier) is a harriers species found in Europe and Asia and Africa regions.

children.name: Circus buffoni
children.description: A large, long-winged harrier of South American grasslands and marshes.

children.name: Circus cinereus
children.description: A pale, grey harrier of Andean highland grasslands and Patagonian steppe.

children.name: Circus melanoleucos
children.description: A striking black-and-white harrier of eastern Asian grasslands.

children.name: Circus macrourus
children.description: A pale, ghostly harrier of Eurasian grasslands and steppes. Near Threatened.

children.name: Circus assimilis
children.description: A distinctive, richly spotted harrier of Australian grasslands; uniquely nests in trees.

children.name: Circus maillardi
children.description: An endangered harrier endemic to Reunion Island.

children.name: Circus ranivorus
children.description: A medium-sized harrier of southern African wetlands; feeds mainly on frogs and small birds.

children.name: Circus approximans
children.description: A large harrier from Australasia; very pale head and heavily streaked body.

children.name: Circus spilonotus
children.description: A large harrier breeding in eastern Asian wetlands from Siberia to Japan.

children.name: Milvus
children.description: Red and Black kites — elegant, long-winged raptors with forked tails and a buoyant, effortless flight.

children.name: Milvus milvus
children.description: One of Europe's most successful raptor reintroductions. British reintroduction now accounts for over 10% of the global population.

children.name: Milvus migrans
children.description: The most numerous raptor in the world. An adaptable scavenger of towns, rubbish dumps, and farmland.

children.name: Milvus aegyptius
children.description: The yellow-billed kite is the Afrotropical counterpart of the black kite, of which it is most often considered a subspecies. However, DNA studies suggest that the yellow-billed kite differs significantly from black kites in the Eurasian clade, and should be considered as a separate, allopatric species.

children.name: Elanus
children.description: Black-shouldered and black-winged kites — small, elegant kites with pearl-grey plumage and distinctive hovering hunting behaviour.

children.name: Elanus caeruleus
children.description: A small, pearl-grey kite with bold black shoulder patches and blood-red eyes.

children.name: Elanus axillaris
children.description: An Australian kite; white with black shoulders and red eyes; hovers over grasslands for rodents.

children.name: Elanus leucurus
children.description: A graceful kite of open country in the Americas; hovers persistently while hunting.

children.name: Elanus scriptus
children.description: A nocturnal kite of inland Australia; black underwing markings form a distinctive 'W' or 'M' pattern.

children.name: Pernis
children.description: Honey buzzards — specialised wasp and hornet nest robbers. More closely related to kites than true buzzards.

children.name: Pernis apivorus
children.description: An enigmatic and variable raptor. A strict long-distance migrant; most European birds winter in west Africa.

children.name: Pernis ptilorhynchus
children.description: A large honey buzzard of Asian forests; prominent crest and long tail. Migrates in large flocks.

children.name: Pernis celebensis
children.description: A honey buzzard of Sulawesi and the Philippines; finely barred underparts.

children.name: Aviceda
children.description: Bazas and cuckoo-hawks — crested, hawk-like raptors of Asian and Australasian forest.

children.name: Aviceda leuphotes
children.description: A striking crested hawk of South and Southeast Asian forests, boldly patterned in black, white, and chestnut.

children.name: Aviceda subcristata
children.description: A crested hawk of Australasian forest; feeds largely on tree frogs, lizards, and large insects.

children.name: Aviceda cuculoides
children.description: A small, crested hawk of sub-Saharan forests; named for its cuckoo-like barred plumage.

children.name: Macheiramphus
children.description: The Bat Hawk — a slender, falcon-like accipitrid specialised for hunting bats and swiftlets at dusk and dawn.

children.name: Macheiramphus alcinus
children.description: A slender, falcon-like raptor specialised for catching bats and swiftlets in flight at twilight.

children.name: Gyps
children.description: Griffon vultures — colonial, cliff-nesting carrion specialists of Africa, Europe, and Asia.

children.name: Gyps fulvus
children.description: Wingspan up to 2.8 m. Locates carcasses by sight and by watching the behaviour of other vultures.

children.name: Gyps africanus
children.description: The most numerous large vulture in Africa. Critically Endangered from poisoning.

children.name: Gyps bengalensis
children.description: Collapsed by over 99.9% due to diclofenac poisoning — one of the most catastrophic vertebrate population crashes.

children.name: Gyps indicus
children.description: Also devastated by diclofenac. Critically Endangered.

children.name: Gyps himalayensis
children.description: The largest Old World vulture, one of the heaviest flying birds with a wingspan reaching 3.1 m.

children.name: Gyps rueppelli
children.description: Critically Endangered. The highest-flying bird ever recorded, confirmed at 11,300 m.

children.name: Gyps coprotheres
children.description: A large vulture from southern Africa; one of the heaviest flying birds.

children.name: Gyps tenuirostris
children.description: Critically endangered; declined by 99% due to veterinary diclofenac in South Asia.

children.name: Aegypius
children.description: The cinereous vulture, one of the largest birds of prey in the world.

children.name: Aegypius monachus
children.description: The largest Old World vulture, soaring over the mountains and steppes of Eurasia. Near Threatened.

children.name: Neophron
children.description: The Egyptian vulture, a small migratory Old World vulture famous for using stones as tools to break open eggs.

children.name: Neophron percnopterus
children.description: A small migratory vulture famous for using stones as tools to crack open ostrich eggs. Endangered.

children.name: Gypaetus
children.description: The bearded vulture or lammergeier, a bone-eating mountain vulture of Eurasia and Africa.

children.name: Gypaetus barbatus
children.description: The lammergeier, a spectacular mountain vulture that subsists largely on bone. Near Threatened.

children.name: Circaetus
children.description: Snake eagles, raptors specialised in hunting reptiles, with broad heads and bare legs protective against snakebite.

children.name: Circaetus gallicus
children.description: A pale, large-headed eagle that hovers over open country hunting snakes and lizards.

children.name: Circaetus pectoralis
children.description: A boldly patterned snake eagle of African savanna with a solid black breast band.

children.name: Circaetus cinereus
children.description: A large, uniformly brown snake eagle of sub-Saharan savanna woodlands.

children.name: Circaetus fasciolatus
children.description: A compact, banded snake eagle of coastal forest in eastern Africa.

children.name: Circaetus cinerascens
children.description: A small snake eagle of West African savannas and woodlands.

children.name: Spilornis
children.description: Serpent eagles — crested, large-eyed raptors of South and Southeast Asian forest specialised in hunting reptiles.

children.name: Spilornis cheela
children.description: A widespread, charismatic eagle of South and Southeast Asian forest with a prominent fan-like crest.

children.name: Spilornis holospilus
children.description: A serpent eagle endemic to the Philippines; dark brown with yellow eyes.

children.name: Clanga
children.description: Spotted eagles, medium-sized dark eagles of wetlands and forests in Eurasia.

children.name: Clanga pomarina
children.description: A forest eagle of eastern Europe that migrates to southern Africa.

children.name: Clanga clanga
children.description: A large dark eagle of wetlands and forests; Vulnerable due to habitat loss and hybridization.

children.name: Hieraaetus
children.description: Hawk-eagles — medium-sized, agile eagles of African and Asian forest and woodland.

children.name: Hieraaetus wahlbergi
children.description: A slender, all-dark eagle of sub-Saharan woodland and savanna.

children.name: Hieraaetus spilogaster
children.description: A powerful, boldly marked eagle of sub-Saharan forest and dense woodland.

children.name: Hieraaetus kienerii
children.description: A compact forest eagle of the Himalayas and Southeast Asia; rufous underparts.

children.name: Hieraaetus ayresii
children.description: A small but powerful eagle of African forests.

children.name: Hieraaetus wekei
children.description: A recently described pygmy eagle from the Horn of Africa.

children.name: Hieraaetus pennatus
children.description: A small migratory eagle; occurs in pale and dark morphs. Winters in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

children.name: Hieraaetus morphnoides
children.description: A small, agile Australian eagle; closely related to the Booted Eagle.

children.name: Terathopius
children.description: The Bateleur — a unique, short-tailed eagle of African savanna whose dramatic acrobatic flight earned it its name.

children.name: Terathopius ecaudatus
children.description: A spectacular short-tailed eagle with a fast, rocking, acrobatic flight style. Near Threatened.

children.name: Lophaetus
children.description: The Long-crested Eagle — a small, distinctive African eagle with a remarkably long, floppy crest.

children.name: Lophaetus occipitalis
children.description: A small African eagle with a distinctively long, floppy crest and bold white wing patches.

children.name: Stephanoaetus
children.description: The African Crowned Eagle — widely considered the most powerful eagle in Africa relative to body size.

children.name: Stephanoaetus coronatus
children.description: The most powerful eagle in Africa relative to body size; kills prey several times its own weight.

children.name: Polemaetus
children.description: The Martial Eagle — the largest eagle in Africa, an apex predator of savanna and open woodland.

children.name: Polemaetus bellicosus
children.description: The largest eagle in Africa, with a wingspan approaching 1.9 m. Vulnerable.

children.name: Haliastur
children.description: Medium-sized kites with broad wings and a distinctive whistling call; foragers of coastal and wetland areas.

children.name: Haliastur sphenurus
children.description: Common across Australia; named for its loud descending whistle.

children.name: Haliastur indus
children.description: A striking white-headed red kite of coastlines; sacred in Hindu culture.

children.name: Ichthyophaga
children.description: Large fishing eagles of Asia; exclusively piscivorous with rough-soled feet for gripping slippery fish.

children.name: Ichthyophaga humilis
children.description: A small fish eagle from Himalayan foothills; dependent on forest streams; Near Threatened.

children.name: Ichthyophaga ichthyaetus
children.description: A medium-sized fish eagle of Southeast Asian wetlands; grey head and chestnut body.

children.name: Elanoides
children.description: Striking black and white kite with an exceptionally long forked tail; almost entirely aerial.

children.name: Elanoides forficatus
children.description: One of the most beautiful raptors; eats insects on the wing; migrates from the US to South America.

children.name: Spizaetus
children.description: Crested forest eagles of the Neotropics and Asia; powerful hunters of medium-sized mammals and birds.

children.name: Spizaetus nipalensis
children.description: A large crested hawk-eagle of Asian mountain forests; powerful feet and striking crest.

children.name: Spizaetus cirrhatus
children.description: A variable Indian and Southeast Asian eagle; both pale and dark morphs exist.

children.name: Spizaetus ornatus
children.description: A spectacular crested Neotropical eagle; black crown and rufous cheeks.

children.name: Spizaetus melanoleucus
children.description: A striking pied eagle of lowland Amazonian forests.

children.name: Spizaetus philippensis
children.description: A medium-sized hawk-eagle endemic to the Philippines; long crest and barred underparts.

children.name: Geranoaetus
children.description: Large, broad-winged raptors of open mountain and savanna habitats in South America, intermediate between Buteo buzzards and true eagles.

children.name: Geranoaetus polyosoma
children.description: A polymorphic Andean hawk; ranges from pale grey to dark morph. Formerly known as Red-backed Hawk.

children.name: Geranoaetus albicaudatus
children.description: A large buzzard of open grasslands from Texas to Argentina; white tail with a black subterminal band.

children.name: Geranoaetus melanoleucus
children.description: A large, powerful raptor of Andean and Patagonian mountains; black breast and grey back.

children.name: Parabuteo
children.description: A distinctive New World genus known for its unique social behaviour — Harris's Hawks hunt in cooperative packs, uncommon among raptors.

children.name: Parabuteo unicinctus
children.description: Famous for cooperative hunting in family groups of 2–7 birds; popular in falconry. Chestnut shoulders and white tail tip.

children.name: Butastur
children.description: Medium-sized, long-winged raptors of Asia and Africa, intermediate in appearance between buzzards and harriers.

children.name: Butastur teesa
children.description: A small buzzard of South Asian dry open country; white eyes and white throat stripe.

children.name: Butastur indicus
children.description: A migratory buzzard breeding in East Asia; grey face and distinctive dark moustachial stripe.

children.name: Butastur liventer
children.description: A little-known buzzard of Southeast Asian open woodlands; rufous wings and white rump.

children.name: Kaupifalco
children.description: A monotypic genus of African raptor resembling a small buzzard but closer to Accipiter. Its specialised diet of reptiles gives it its common name.

children.name: Kaupifalco monogrammicus
children.description: A small raptor of sub-Saharan woodlands; grey with a white throat and black vertical stripe.

children.name: Buteogallus
children.description: Neotropical raptors ranging from dark forest-dwelling species to open-country savanna hawks. Most have very broad wings and short tails.

children.name: Buteogallus anthracinus
children.description: A dark, broad-winged hawk of coastal mangroves and riparian woodland; broad white tail band.

children.name: Buteogallus urubitinga
children.description: A large, long-legged black hawk of lowland forests and wetlands; white tail base.

children.name: Buteogallus meridionalis
children.description: A rufous-brown hawk of open country and marshes; long legs and rufous underparts.

children.name: Buteogallus schistaceus
children.description: A medium-sized slate-grey hawk of Amazonian riversides.

children.name: Buteogallus solitarius
children.description: A large, dark forest eagle of montane Central and South America; Near Threatened.

children.name: Leucopternis
children.description: Neotropical forest hawks with broad, rounded wings and short tails. Most species are strikingly patterned in black, white, and grey.

children.name: Leucopternis melanops
children.description: A small forest hawk of Amazonia; black face mask and white breast.

children.name: Leucopternis semiplumbeus
children.description: A striking hawk of Central and South American lowland forests; orange cere and legs.

children.name: Leucopternis kuhli
children.description: A grey-and-white hawk of Amazonian forests; bold white brow and black tail band.

children.name: Nisaetus
children.description: Crested forest eagles of tropical Asia, formerly merged with Spizaetus. They are powerful hunters of mammals and birds in forest canopies.

children.name: Nisaetus lanceolatus
children.description: A medium-sized hawk-eagle endemic to Sulawesi and its satellites; long crest and barred underparts.

children.name: Nisaetus bartelsi
children.description: A rare, endangered crested eagle endemic to Java; the national bird of Indonesia.

children.name: Nisaetus alboniger
children.description: A black-and-white crested eagle of Southeast Asian forests; prominent black crest.

children.name: Ictinaetus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Black Eagle, a large raptor of Asian tropical forests distinguished by its very long, broad wings and distinctive flight silhouette.

children.name: Ictinaetus malaiensis
children.description: A large, dark forest eagle of South and Southeast Asia; very long and broad wings with a distinctive straight trailing edge.

children.name: Gampsonyx
children.description: A monotypic genus of tiny Neotropical raptor, the smallest diurnal bird of prey in the Americas. Superficially resembles the black-shouldered kites of Elanus.

children.name: Gampsonyx swainsonii
children.description: The smallest raptor in the Americas; pearl-grey and rufous with bright yellow eyes.

children.name: Sarcogyps
children.description: A monotypic vulture genus of South Asia; the bare red head and neck give it a distinctive appearance. Also known as the Pondicherry Vulture.

children.name: Sarcogyps calvus
children.description: A medium-sized vulture with a striking red head and neck. Critically Endangered due to diclofenac poisoning.

children.name: Trigonoceps
children.description: A monotypic African vulture genus, distinguished by its white head and sharply triangular bare patch on the crown.

children.name: Trigonoceps occipitalis
children.description: A medium-sized African vulture with a white head and brightly coloured bill. Critically Endangered.

children.name: Torgos
children.description: The Lappet-faced Vulture is one of the largest and most powerful vultures in Africa, dominating carcasses with its massive bill and aggressive disposition.

children.name: Torgos tracheliotos
children.description: A massive, powerful vulture with large fleshy lappets on the sides of the head. Endangered.

children.name: Necrosyrtes
children.description: A small, scruffy-looking vulture of sub-Saharan Africa and the only member of its genus. It is the most terrestrial of African vultures.

children.name: Necrosyrtes monachus
children.description: A small vulture with a slender bill; common in towns and villages. Critically Endangered due to poisoning and habitat loss.

children.name: Harpia
children.description: The Harpy Eagle is one of the largest and most powerful eagles in the world, with enormous talons capable of crushing skulls.

children.name: Harpia harpyja
children.description: One of the world's largest eagles; massive black-and-white raptor of Neotropical rainforest. Near Threatened.

children.name: Morphnus
children.description: A monotypic genus of Neotropical forest eagle, closely related to the Harpy Eagle but smaller and with a slenderer build.

children.name: Morphnus guianensis
children.description: A large grey forest eagle of Central and South America; prominent crest. Near Threatened.

children.name: Pithecophaga
children.description: The Philippine Eagle is one of the largest and most critically endangered eagles in the world, endemic to the Philippines.

children.name: Pithecophaga jefferyi
children.description: One of the world's largest eagles; brown and white with a spectacular shaggy crest. Critically Endangered.

children.name: Polyboroides
children.description: Medium-sized African raptors with a unique double-jointed leg adaptation allowing them to extract prey from tree cavities and crevices.

children.name: Polyboroides typus
children.description: A grey raptor with a bare yellow face; feeds on nests, palm fruits, and insects.

children.name: Polyboroides radiatus
children.description: A larger harrier-hawk endemic to Madagascar; brownish-grey with a bare yellow face.

children.name: Gypohierax
children.description: A unique vulture that feeds primarily on the fruit of oil palms, one of the few primarily frugivorous raptors.

children.name: Gypohierax angolensis
children.description: A mostly white vulture of African forests and mangroves; uniquely adapted to eat oil palm nuts.

children.name: Rostrhamus
children.description: A highly specialised kite with a slender, hook-tipped bill adapted exclusively for extracting snails from their shells.

children.name: Rostrhamus sociabilis
children.description: A dark grey kite with a slender, deeply hooked bill; feeds almost exclusively on apple snails.

children.name: Helicolestes
children.description: A monotypic South American kite closely related to Rostrhamus, also specialised for snail-eating.

children.name: Helicolestes hamatus
children.description: A dark grey kite of Amazonian wetlands; hooked bill for extracting snails.
